item.title: ClickFix SmartRAT and Meow Campaigns, FortiBleed Leak, and UK CNI State Threats
item.description: This digest is dominated by credential-driven intrusion activity, social-engineered initial access, and attacker infrastructure visibility. Reporting spans AI-assisted ClickFix campaigns against Windows and macOS users, large-scale Fortinet VPN credential exposure, open-source C2 fingerprinting,...
item.content:encoded:
This digest is dominated by credential-driven intrusion activity, social-engineered initial access, and attacker infrastructure visibility. Reporting spans AI-assisted ClickFix campaigns against Windows and macOS users, large-scale Fortinet VPN credential exposure, open-source C2 fingerprinting, and renewed warnings about state prepositioning in critical infrastructure. Zscaler ThreatLabz reported a March 2026 ClickFix campaign using an AI-generated typosquatting site impersonating a Brazilian bank to deliver SmartRAT. The lure presented a fraudulent Cloudflare CAPTCHA followed by a fullscreen fake BSOD recovery prompt, pressuring victims into executing a PowerShell command that downloaded the malware. SmartRAT provides encrypted C2, remote screen and input control, keylogging, fake bank overlays, QR code interception, and persistence via scheduled tasks and a Windows service. SmartRAT is financially focused, monitoring window titles associated with Brazilian banking, payment, and cryptocurrency activity and alerting operators when victims interact with targeted services. ThreatLabz also found a weakness in the SmartRAT web C2 panel in which client-side authentication could be bypassed by setting arbitrary localStorage values. Netskope Threat Labs separately described a macOS ClickFix campaign observed on May 31, 2026 that delivered an upgraded AppleScript payload labeled “Meow (DEBUG).” The infection chain remained fileless until persistence, using a victim-executed curl command to fetch a compressed stager and pipe AppleScript into osascript memory. The payload checks for Russian keyboard input, steals credentials, cookies, keychain contents, crypto wallet data, messaging sessions, files, gaming data, and Apple Notes, then persists as com.apple.accountsd for recurring C2 beacons and arbitrary command execution. A newly identified leak dubbed FortiBleed exposed apparent Fortinet and FortiGate VPN credentials for 73,932 firewall URLs worldwide. Researcher Bob Diachenko said the exposed server contained usernames, email addresses, and plaintext passwords, with references to major organizations including Chevron, Samsung, Foxconn, Comcast, AT&T, Mercedes-Benz, Toyota, Sinopec, and State Grid. Kevin Beaumont reported confirming the authenticity of some administrative credentials and said the dataset appeared to originate from exported Fortinet configurations. The FortiBleed reporting attributed the activity to a Russian-speaking, multi-operator threat group that allegedly made about 1.16 billion credential attempts against 320,777 FortiGate targets and 2.1 billion attempts against 163,650 Microsoft SQL Server systems. In parallel, Zscaler’s 2026 phishing reporting found that overall phishing volume in its cloud fell 20% year over year, while remaining campaigns became more targeted, AI-powered, and difficult to distinguish from legitimate activity. Zscaler identified 413,524 AI-generated site instances during the analysis period and assessed 9% as malicious. Government phishing attempts increased 50% year over year to 138.5 million hits in 2025, while healthcare saw lower phishing counts but 95.2% of phishing activity over encrypted channels. Separate account takeover reporting emphasized attacker adaptation to MFA through fatigue tactics, adversary-in-the-middle frameworks, browser-in-the-middle kits, session hijacking, and credential theft from unmanaged or compromised endpoints. Richard Horne, chief executive of the UK National Cyber Security Centre, said the agency handled more than 200 incidents affecting UK critical infrastructure and its supporting ecosystem in the year to May, with roughly 75% believed to involve state actors. He warned that adversaries are prepositioning inside technology underpinning British critical national infrastructure to enable rapid exploitation and mass disruption during conflict. Horne cited Volt Typhoon as the clearest example of this prepositioning model. The remarks coincided with UK government work on the Cyber Security and Resilience Bill and an expected National Cyber Action Plan. Horne framed cybersecurity as a contest against capable opponents rather than only a risk-management discipline, emphasizing capability relative to adversaries. Ukraine has also been granted access to the European Union’s Cybersecurity Reserve, allowing Kyiv to request EU-approved expertise during major cyberattacks. The reserve, managed by ENISA, provides incident response, digital forensics, technical expertise, and recovery support when incidents exceed national capacity. Ukrainian officials said the country already shares intelligence on Russian hacking techniques with European partners and participates in joint cybercrime investigations and attribution work. Censys analyzed AdaptixC2, an open-source C2 framework whose default configuration exposes identifiable HTTP headers such as Server: AdaptixC2 and Adaptix-Version: v1.2 on unauthenticated error responses. As of 16 June 2026, Censys tracked 412 web properties across 236 hosts using default or near-default AdaptixC2 settings. The research identified beacon listener clusters hosted by Hivelocity, M247, and PSB Hosting, as well as one host serving AdaptixC2 payloads from an open HTTP directory. The exposed directory included a Linux installer that deployed timesync.bin and established 12 persistence mechanisms. Censys noted that even operators who changed the default Server header could remain detectable if they left the default 404 response body string intact. Cato Networks reported on a French-speaking attacker known as “Poisson,” who compromised a small French automotive business, installed a keylogger, and stole banking and email credentials. Before his Havoc C2 server went offline, the attacker installed OpenSSH Server and Tailscale on a victim machine to preserve access outside the original C2 channel. Cato captured 339 commands over 33 days after the operator left SSH keys and a step-by-step playbook in an open storage bucket, with the intrusion involving VBScript, PowerShell, .NET loaders, Havoc Demon, scheduled-task persistence, Explorer.exe injection, RustDesk, and a Python keylogger. Silent Push described the preparation phase as the period in which threat actors build and stage domains, servers, DNS records, certificates, and testing environments before a campaign becomes active. The company argued that SIEMs, EDR platforms, blocklists, and standard threat intelligence feeds generally rely on observed malicious events or known-bad indicators, leaving this phase underdetected. The article stated that traditional threat data captures roughly the final 5% of the offensive lifecycle, when infrastructure is already operational. Silent Push said its approach maps DNS, WHOIS, certificate data, and host configurations to identify Indicators of Future Attack, claiming an average early-detection lead time of 104 days ahead of traditional SIEM alerts and more than 300 days in some APT cases.

FortiBleed leak exposes Fortinet VPN credentials for 73,000 devices. — Must Read

A leaked dataset dubbed FortiBleed appears to expose valid Fortinet/FortiGate VPN and admin credentials for about 74,000 firewall URLs worldwide, including major enterprises and government-linked organizations. Affected organizations should immediately rotate Fortinet passwords, enable MFA, and review logs for compromise.

  • A newly discovered data leak dubbed FortiBleed exposed what appears to be Fortinet and FortiGate VPN credentials for 73,932 firewall URLs at organizations worldwide.
  • Security researcher Bob Diachenko said he found a server containing usernames, email addresses, and plaintext passwords, with entries referencing companies such as Chevron, Samsung, Foxconn, Comcast, AT&T, Mercedes-Benz, Toyota, Sinopec, and State Grid.
  • Diachenko claimed the operation was conducted by a Russian-speaking multi-operator threat group that made about 1.16 billion credential attempts against 320,777 FortiGate targets and 2.1 billion attempts against 163,650 Microsoft SQL Server systems.
  • Cybersecurity researcher Kevin Beaumont said he confirmed the authenticity of some admin logins and passwords and found the dataset appears to have originated from exported Fortinet configurations.
  • Organizations in the dataset are advised to rotate Fortinet VPN and administrative passwords, enforce MFA, examine gateway logs for suspicious activity, and monitor for exposed employee credentials.

macOS ClickFix Lures Deploy AppleScript Stealer & Persistent RAT — Must Read

Netskope found a new macOS ClickFix campaign that tricks users into running a fileless command, then deploys the “Meow” AppleScript stealer plus a persistent RAT. The malware steals credentials, browser and wallet data, modifies desktop crypto wallet apps, and installs a fake Apple accounts service for ongoing remote command execution.

  • Netskope Threat Labs observed a new macOS ClickFix campaign on May 31, 2026 that delivers an upgraded AppleScript-based payload self-labeled “Meow (DEBUG)” with remote access trojan capabilities.
  • The infection chain is fileless until persistence is established, with victims socially engineered into running a curl command that fetches a gzip-compressed stager and pipes a second-stage AppleScript directly into osascript memory.
  • The stage 1 loader checks the macOS keyboard layout for Russian input in com.apple.HIToolbox.plist, reports a cis_blocked event to the C2 if Russian input is found, and exits silently.
  • The payload steals the user’s login password through a fake System Preferences dialog and collects browser credentials, cookies, keychain contents, crypto wallet data, messaging sessions, gaming account data, files, and Apple Notes.
  • After exfiltration, the malware can overwrite app.asar files in selected desktop cryptocurrency wallets and installs a LaunchDaemon or LaunchAgent labeled com.apple.accountsd that beacons to the C2 every 60 seconds for arbitrary command execution.

ClickFix Campaign Generated Via AI Delivers SmartRAT — Must Read

Zscaler ThreatLabz found an AI-generated ClickFix phishing site impersonating a Brazilian bank that tricks users into running PowerShell and installs SmartRAT, a banking RAT with remote control, keylogging, fake bank overlays, QR-code interception, and persistence. The campaign shows how AI-built lures can rapidly enable convincing malware delivery at scale.

  • Zscaler ThreatLabz observed a March 2026 ClickFix campaign using an AI-generated typosquatting website impersonating a Brazilian bank to deliver the PowerShell-based SmartRAT malware.
  • The fake website displayed a fraudulent Cloudflare CAPTCHA and then a fullscreen fake BSOD recovery prompt to pressure victims into running a PowerShell command that downloaded and executed the malware.
  • SmartRAT supports encrypted command-and-control communications, remote screen and input control, credential theft through keylogging and fake bank-branded overlays, QR code interception, and persistence through scheduled tasks and a Windows service.
  • SmartRAT monitors window titles for Brazilian banking, payment, and cryptocurrency-related activity and can alert the operator when a victim interacts with targeted financial services.
  • ThreatLabz found that the SmartRAT web-based C2 panel had a client-side authentication weakness that allowed the login screen to be bypassed by setting arbitrary localStorage values.

Hostile states behind three-quarters of attacks on Britain's critical infrastructure, cyber chief warns — Should Read

The UK’s cyber chief warned that hostile states are behind about three-quarters of cyber incidents affecting Britain’s critical infrastructure, with adversaries already prepositioning for potential future conflict. He urged organizations to treat cybersecurity as an ongoing contest against capable opponents, not just a risk-management exercise.

  • Richard Horne, chief executive of the National Cyber Security Centre, said the agency handled more than 200 incidents affecting UK critical infrastructure and its supporting ecosystem in the year to May, with about 75% believed to be caused by state actors.
  • Horne warned that adversaries are prepositioning within technology underpinning British critical national infrastructure to enable rapid exploitation and mass disruption during a conflict.
  • Horne cited Volt Typhoon, a Chinese state-linked campaign exposed against U.S. infrastructure, as the clearest example of this prepositioning tactic.
  • Horne argued that cybersecurity should be treated as a contest to be fought rather than a risk to be managed, saying the relevant benchmark is capability against an opponent rather than industry peers.
  • The speech came as the UK government moves to pass the Cyber Security and Resilience Bill and plans to introduce a new National Cyber Action Plan expected in early July.

AdaptixC2: Fingerprinting an Open-Source C2 Framework at Scale — Should Read

AdaptixC2’s default HTTP headers and 404 page make many deployed teamservers and beacon listeners easy to fingerprint at internet scale, with Censys tracking 412 web properties across 236 hosts. Defenders can detect both default and lightly modified deployments using the provided header, body, DNS, and Censys threat-label queries.

  • AdaptixC2’s default configuration sets the HTTP headers `Server: AdaptixC2` and `Adaptix-Version: v1.2` on unauthenticated error responses, making default deployments identifiable through passive scanning.
  • As of 16 June 2026, Censys tracked 412 web properties across 236 hosts running AdaptixC2 with default or near-default settings.
  • Censys identified three beacon listener clusters hosted by Hivelocity, M247, and PSB Hosting that used adjacent IP ranges, randomized or shared high ports, and identical default TLS certificates.
  • One host, 2.26.229[.]254, served AdaptixC2 payloads from an open HTTP directory, including a Linux installer that deployed `timesync.bin` and established 12 persistence mechanisms.
  • The article states that operators who changed the `Server` header could still be detected because they left the default 404 body string, `You need to enter the correct connection details.`, intact.

What the ThreatLabz 2026 Phishing and Initial Access Report Means for the Public Sector — Should Read

ThreatLabz says phishing targeting the public sector is becoming more AI-generated, targeted, and capable of bypassing legacy defenses, even as overall volume declines. Government, healthcare, and education should prioritize encrypted traffic inspection, phishing-resistant authentication, reduced exposed attack surface, and broader visibility beyond email.

  • The ThreatLabz 2026 Phishing and Initial Access Report found that overall phishing volume in the Zscaler cloud fell 20% year over year, while remaining campaigns became more targeted, AI-powered, and harder to distinguish from legitimate activity.
  • ThreatLabz identified 413,524 AI-generated site instances during the analysis period and flagged 9% of them as malicious, with platforms such as Manus AI, BlackBox AI, and Anything AI used to create phishing infrastructure quickly.
  • Phishing attempts against the government sector increased 50% year over year, reaching 138.5 million phishing hits in 2025 and making government the third-most targeted industry overall in the Zscaler cloud.
  • Healthcare had comparatively lower phishing hit counts in 2025, but the article states that 95.2% of all phishing activity was delivered over encrypted channels and that adversary-in-the-middle and browser-in-the-middle phishing kits can capture credentials and MFA tokens during active login flows.
  • Phishing attempts against education organizations dropped 65.6% year over year, but education still experienced roughly 1.6 billion encrypted attack hits, representing 6% of encrypted attack activity in the Zscaler cloud.

Junior Hacker Used Tailscale and OpenSSH to Keep Access After His C2 Went Offline — May Read

A low-skill attacker maintained access to a French small business even after his Havoc C2 went offline by installing OpenSSH and Tailscale as a separate backdoor. The key lesson is that taking down C2 infrastructure is not enough remediation unless defenders also hunt for alternate persistence paths like legitimate remote-access tools, scheduled tasks, and keyloggers.

  • A French-speaking attacker known as "Poisson" compromised a small French automotive business, installed a keylogger, and stole banking and email credentials.
  • Before his Havoc command-and-control server went offline, Poisson installed OpenSSH Server and Tailscale on a victim machine to maintain access outside the C2 channel.
  • Cato Networks captured 339 commands over 33 days after the operator left SSH keys and a step-by-step playbook in an open storage bucket.
  • The intrusion used a VBScript stager, PowerShell loader, .NET loader, Havoc Demon agent, scheduled-task persistence, Explorer.exe injection, RustDesk, and a Python keylogger.
  • Cato Networks recommended hunting for OpenSSH Server on Windows workstations, tailscale.exe on unexpected machines, ssh -R reverse tunnels, suspicious wscript.exe activity, high-privilege scheduled tasks launching script interpreters, powercfg standby-timeout changes, and DuckDNS usage.

EU grants Ukraine access to cybersecurity reserve for major attacks — May Read

Ukraine has been granted access to the EU Cybersecurity Reserve, allowing it to request EU-approved experts for major cyberattacks that exceed its own response capacity. The move deepens EU-Ukraine security cooperation and integrates Kyiv further into Europe’s collective cyber defense framework.

  • Ukraine has been granted access to the European Union's Cybersecurity Reserve, allowing Kyiv to call on EU-approved experts during major cyberattacks.
  • The EU Cybersecurity Reserve is managed by ENISA and provides incident response, technical expertise, and recovery support to participating countries facing cyber incidents beyond their own capabilities.
  • Ukraine will be able to request European assistance for digital forensics, incident response, and system recovery if a cyberattack exceeds the capacity of its own teams.
  • Natalia Tkachuk said Ukraine already shares intelligence on Russian hacking techniques with European partners and participates in joint cybercrime investigations and attribution efforts.
  • Ukraine hopes its cybersecurity companies will eventually be able to join the Cyber Reserve as trusted service providers under the EU's Cyber Solidarity Act.

Why Account Takeovers Are Rising and How to Stop Them — Optional

Account takeover attacks are rising because attackers increasingly steal credentials, hijack sessions, bypass MFA, and exploit unmanaged or compromised devices. The article argues organizations need continuous identity and device trust checks throughout sessions, not just authentication at login.

  • Account takeover attacks are increasing because organizations manage many human and non-human identities across cloud services, SaaS applications, endpoints, and remote environments while losing visibility into access and trust.
  • Attackers are adapting to multi-factor authentication by using MFA fatigue, adversary-in-the-middle frameworks, and session hijacking tools to steal authenticated session tokens or gain approval.
  • Credential phishing remains common and has become more sophisticated through legitimate hosting services, trusted domains, reverse proxies, and AI-generated content that mimic real login portals.
  • Personal laptops, unmanaged mobile devices, and compromised endpoints expand the attack surface by giving attackers opportunities to harvest credentials, browser-stored passwords, and authenticated session cookies.
  • The article says organizations need continuous verification of device posture, session risk, and behavioral signals throughout the access lifecycle rather than relying only on successful authentication.

What Is the Preparation Phase? The Part of the Attack Timeline Most Tools Miss — Optional

The article argues that most security tools detect threats too late because attackers spend weeks or months preparing domains, servers, DNS, and certificates before any malicious activity appears. Silent Push promotes its preemptive approach for identifying this staged infrastructure before campaigns launch.

  • The preparation phase is the period when a threat actor builds and stages attack infrastructure such as domains, servers, DNS records, certificates, and testing environments before a campaign goes live.
  • Conventional tools such as SIEMs, EDR platforms, blocklists, and standard threat intelligence feeds depend on observed malicious events or known-bad indicators, so they usually do not detect preparation-phase activity.
  • The article states that traditional threat data captures roughly the final 5% of the offensive lifecycle, when infrastructure is already operational and a malicious campaign is already running.
  • Silent Push says its platform maps internet infrastructure across DNS, WHOIS, certificate data, and host configurations to generate Indicators of Future Attack for staging environments before they are used.
  • The article claims Silent Push provides an average early-detection lead time of 104 days ahead of a traditional SIEM alert, with some APT campaigns exceeding 300 days.

item.title: Mastra npm Takeover, CVE-2026-48907 Exploitation, and Crypto Hijacker Campaigns
item.description: This digest window is dominated by software supply chain compromise, active exploitation of internet-facing web components, and financially motivated campaigns abusing trust signals and weak access controls. Reporting also highlights operational disruption across major collaboration and...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by software supply chain compromise, active exploitation of internet-facing web components, and financially motivated campaigns abusing trust signals and weak access controls. Reporting also highlights operational disruption across major collaboration and productivity platforms, alongside ongoing concerns about exposure prioritization and public-sector cyber capacity. Mend reported a compromise of more than 140 packages in the @mastra npm scope, where an attacker republished packages with a new dependency on easy-day-js. Version 1.11.22 of easy-day-js introduced an obfuscated postinstall dropper that retrieved a second-stage Node.js payload from 23.254.164.92:8000 and launched it as a detached hidden process communicating with 23.254.164.123:443. The second stage was described as harvesting environment secrets and cryptocurrency wallet data, with affected installations treated in the reporting as fully compromised. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to address CVE-2026-48907, a maximum-severity improper access control flaw in the Widget Factory Joomla Content Editor plugin. The JCE security team said the vulnerability allows unauthenticated PHP upload and execution, is actively exploited, has public exploit code available, and is being targeted through automated attacks. The flaw was fixed in JCE Pro 2.9.99.6, while the vendor noted that updating does not remediate sites already compromised before patching. Broader exposure data from Intruder underscored why these classes of issues remain operationally significant. Across 3,000 attack surfaces, 60% of organizations had at least one HTTP panel exposed, 42% had an internet-reachable database, and 30% exposed files or information that should not have been public. MySQL, Postgres, API documentation, WordPress admin panels, and RDP were among the most common findings. Check Point Research analyzed a Rust-based cryptocurrency clipboard hijacker campaign disguised as Solana and Pump.fun sniper bots, Aviator Predictor tools, and crash-game predictors. The actor used a WordPress phishing site as a landing hub and promoted payloads through GitHub, SourceForge, YouTube, crypto forums, and posts on legitimate news websites. The campaign also inflated trust signals using coordinated or fake GitHub engagement, SourceForge ratings and downloads, YouTube activity, and benign VirusTotal votes and comments. The Windows payload persisted by copying itself to %APPDATA%\\silke\\silke.exe and creating a Startup folder shortcut, while the macOS variant used an unlocker.command script to remove quarantine attributes and installed a LaunchAgent plist with RunAtLoad and KeepAlive. Both monitored clipboard or pasteboard contents and replaced cryptocurrency wallet addresses with attacker-controlled addresses. IRONSCALES reported a French-language invoice notification sent through Mailgun from an Ecocert accounting subdomain that passed SPF, DKIM, and DMARC authentication. The email linked to an Upflow billing portal URL containing a JWT with portal.view permissions, a customer identifier, and a 60-day expiration, allowing anyone with the tokenized link to access the portal without credentials. The invoice requested a 380 euro payment and included French IBAN and BIC details, while a tracking pixel confirmed mailbox activity. BleepingComputer reported that India restricted Telegram access nationwide until June 22 after the National Testing Agency said the platform was used to sell access to leaked exam materials. The disruption extended beyond India, including to users in the UAE, after AS18101 announced Telegram prefixes and the route leaked globally via FLAG Telecom. Researchers confirmed the route hijack but said they found no evidence that the wider disruption was deliberate, while civil society groups criticized the block as disproportionate. Microsoft confirmed it is investigating Office launch issues after Windows updates released on or after June 9, 2026. The issue affects third-party applications using OLE automation to open Office apps or documents, including Word, Excel, PowerPoint, and Access, and may fail without an error message. User reports named affected applications such as CCH Engagement, Zotero, Workpaper Manager, Dentrix, and Softdent. Operationally, The Record covered Sen. Mark Warner’s letter warning of CISA staffing cuts, short-staffed regional divisions, and the disbanding of the MS-ISAC program supporting state and local critical infrastructure operators. Separately, Netskope introduced its Catalyst MSP/SP Program for managed SSE and SASE delivery, while a Hacker News article framed adversarial exposure validation as a way to distinguish theoretical exposure from reachable and consequential risk within Continuous Threat Exposure Management.

item.title: Mastra npm Compromise, RoguePlanet CVE-2026-50656, and AI Tool Data Theft
item.description: This digest is dominated by software supply chain and developer ecosystem risk, with multiple reports detailing compromised npm packages, malicious IDE plugins, and browser extensions harvesting AI-related data. The coverage also includes a publicly disclosed Microsoft Defender...
item.content:encoded: This digest is dominated by software supply chain and developer ecosystem risk, with multiple reports detailing compromised npm packages, malicious IDE plugins, and browser extensions harvesting AI-related data. The coverage also includes a publicly disclosed Microsoft Defender elevation-of-privilege zero-day and a confirmed Kodak data breach claimed by ShinyHunters. Multiple reports from Aikido, Socket, and The Hacker News describe a coordinated npm supply chain attack affecting more than 140 packages in the @mastra namespace. The malicious publications occurred on June 17, when a hijacked npm account belonging to a legitimate former Mastra contributor mass-published compromised versions across the scope within a short window. The affected packages reportedly did not embed malware directly, but instead added a dependency on easy-day-js, a malicious clone or typosquat of dayjs. The easy-day-js package used a postinstall hook to execute an obfuscated setup script during installation. That script downloaded a second-stage payload from attacker-controlled infrastructure, including 23[.]254[.]164[.]92:8000, wrote it to a randomly named JavaScript file in the operating system temporary directory, spawned it as a detached background process, and deleted itself. Socket reported that the loader also disabled TLS certificate validation before retrieving the payload. The recovered second-stage malware was described as a cross-platform Node.js tasking client with persistence mechanisms for Windows, macOS, and Linux. Its collection logic targeted host reconnaissance data, browser history from Chrome, Edge, and Brave, and more than 160 cryptocurrency wallet browser extensions. Researchers noted similarities to an earlier axios compromise, including the use of a malicious dependency, a clean decoy version preceding the malicious release, automatic postinstall execution, and self-deleting droppers. Aikido Security reported a coordinated malware campaign on the JetBrains Marketplace involving at least 15 malicious plugins masquerading as AI coding assistants. The plugins reportedly functioned as advertised while secretly exfiltrating AI provider API keys entered by users, including keys for OpenAI, SiliconFlow, and DeepSeek. The stolen keys were sent over plaintext HTTP to 39.107.60[.]51. Two of the reported JetBrains plugins, CodeGPT AI Assistant and DeepSeek AI Assist, were listed with more than 25,000 downloads each, although the authenticity of those counts was unclear. The campaign reflects continued attacker interest in developer environments where AI service credentials may be stored, entered, or reused across workflows. Separately, researcher Jean-Marie R. reported that two Chrome ad blocker extensions, Smart Adblocker and Adblock for Browser, captured users’ conversations with AI chatbots. The extensions reportedly provided genuine ad-blocking capability while operating an undisclosed telemetry channel that collected full AI conversation histories, model usage, and subscription tier metadata across services including ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Copilot, Perplexity, DeepSeek, Grok, and Meta AI. Microsoft confirmed it is working on a security update for a Microsoft Defender zero-day vulnerability publicly referred to as RoguePlanet and now tracked as CVE-2026-50656. The issue is described as an elevation-of-privilege flaw in the Microsoft Malware Protection Engine involving a race condition. Researcher Nightmare Eclipse published a proof-of-concept exploit in a self-hosted Git repository and claimed the exploit affects fully patched Windows 10 and Windows 11 systems. According to the reporting, the exploit can spawn command prompts with SYSTEM privileges and may work regardless of whether Defender real-time protection is enabled. The disclosure is also tied to an ongoing dispute between the researcher and Microsoft regarding bug bounty and vulnerability disclosure practices. Kodak confirmed that an unauthorized third party temporarily accessed a limited amount of company data. The company said it engaged external cybersecurity experts, is investigating what data was accessed and copied, and is working with law enforcement. Kodak also stated that it is confident there is no threat to its systems or operations. The ShinyHunters extortion group claimed responsibility for the incident on its dark web leak site. The group alleged it stole more than 2.2 million records containing customer personally identifiable information and internal corporate data, and threatened to leak the data on June 18, 2026.

item.title: Mastra npm Supply Chain Attack Backdoors 140+ Packages via easy-day-js
item.description: The coverage window is dominated by a significant npm supply chain compromise affecting the Mastra AI framework ecosystem. Reporting from StepSecurity describes a coordinated package manipulation incident in which a typosquatted dependency was introduced across more than 140 packages under the...
item.content:encoded: The coverage window is dominated by a significant npm supply chain compromise affecting the Mastra AI framework ecosystem. Reporting from StepSecurity describes a coordinated package manipulation incident in which a typosquatted dependency was introduced across more than 140 packages under the @mastra npm organization, exposing downstream development and CI/CD environments to a postinstall-based payload chain. The incident is notable for its scale, its targeting of an AI framework package namespace, and its use of a staged delivery mechanism designed to execute during package installation. The affected Mastra packages reportedly had combined weekly downloads exceeding 1.1 million, making this a high-impact software supply chain event for organizations consuming JavaScript and AI development dependencies. StepSecurity reported that on June 17, 2026, an attacker compromised the @mastra npm organization and added a dependency named easy-day-js across more than 140 Mastra packages. The package name closely resembled the legitimate dayjs library, indicating a typosquatting approach intended to blend into JavaScript dependency trees and exploit developer familiarity with common date/time libraries. The malicious package was staged across versions. Version 1.11.21 of easy-day-js was reportedly published as a clean bait package, while version 1.11.22 introduced an obfuscated postinstall dropper. This sequencing is consistent with supply chain tradecraft in which an attacker first establishes a benign-looking package history before pushing a malicious update after trust or dependency inclusion has been achieved. The reported malicious behavior centered on npm’s postinstall lifecycle hook. According to StepSecurity, the obfuscated script attempted to download a second-stage payload from https://23.254.164.92:8000/update/49890878, spawn it as a detached Node.js process, and then delete setup.cjs after execution. This design indicates an effort to move beyond package-level code execution into a more persistent or independently running payload while reducing local forensic artifacts tied to the installer script. The use of a direct IP address and nonstandard port for payload retrieval suggests attacker-controlled infrastructure rather than a commodity package registry-hosted payload. StepSecurity also reported that, during a controlled GitHub Actions test, its Harden Runner in block mode intercepted and blocked the outbound connection to 23.254.164.92:8000, preventing the stage-two payload from being retrieved in that environment. The reported scale of the compromised package set places this incident among high-concern npm ecosystem supply chain events, particularly because it affected an AI framework namespace with broad downstream usage. StepSecurity stated that the affected Mastra packages had more than 1.1 million combined weekly downloads, and assessed that anyone who installed an @mastra package on the day of the compromise should consider the environment compromised. For threat hunters and detection engineers, the reporting provides concrete infrastructure and behavioral details: the package name easy-day-js, malicious version 1.11.22, postinstall execution, retrieval from 23.254.164.92:8000, and execution of a detached Node.js second stage. The incident reinforces ongoing adversary interest in developer ecosystems, CI/CD workflows, and trusted open-source dependency chains as distribution mechanisms for code execution at scale.

item.title: npm shai_hulululud Targets AI Malware Scanners With Prompt Injection
item.description: The coverage window is dominated by a software supply chain finding involving an npm package that appears designed to interfere with AI-assisted malware analysis rather than deliver a conventional credential-stealing payload. Socket Threat Research reported that the package,...
item.content:encoded: The coverage window is dominated by a software supply chain finding involving an npm package that appears designed to interfere with AI-assisted malware analysis rather than deliver a conventional credential-stealing payload. Socket Threat Research reported that the package, shaihulululud@1.0.48596, uses prompt-injection-style content, context flooding, and layered obfuscation to complicate automated review. Socket Threat Research identified shaihulululud@1.0.48596 as a suspicious npm package that differs from recent Mini Shai-Hulud, Miasma, and Hades activity. Rather than carrying the credential theft behavior associated with those campaigns, the package appears aimed at disrupting AI-based malware scanners and analysis workflows. The package includes an approximately 9.28 MB index.js file containing policy-triggering prompt text, fake system override instructions, and tens of thousands of repeated comment lines. This material is not executed by JavaScript, but it can influence LLM-based scanners by consuming context windows, causing truncation, triggering refusals, or shifting attention away from executable logic. Socket also reported that executable JavaScript was appended after the large prompt and comment content. Researchers described the code as obfuscated through a character-code array, a ROT-style substitution function, eval usage, and a second AES-encrypted layer, and classified the package as protestware or potentially unwanted behavior. The report highlights a growing concern around adversarial content embedded in software packages to manipulate AI-enabled security tooling. In this case, the malicious or unwanted behavior is less about exploiting runtime environments directly and more about shaping how automated scanners interpret, summarize, or fail to analyze the package. The technique illustrates how non-executed source content can still have operational impact when analysis pipelines rely on large language models. Prompt-like instructions and repeated filler can create conditions where analysis output is incomplete, refused, or biased toward irrelevant text while executable code remains buried near the end of the file. This finding is notable for threat hunters, detection engineers, and SOC analysts tracking software supply chain abuse because it reflects a shift in attacker experimentation toward the analysis layer itself. The package demonstrates how npm ecosystem threats may increasingly combine traditional JavaScript obfuscation with content designed specifically to degrade AI-assisted triage and malware classification.

item.title: JetBrains AI Key Theft, Vertex AI Bucket Squatting, Rokarolla, and REDCap Exposure
item.description: This coverage window was dominated by software supply chain and developer-platform risk, especially where AI tooling intersects with credentials, model workflows, and autonomous agents. Reporting also highlighted continued abuse of trusted distribution channels, mobile banking malware, and exposed...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window was dominated by software supply chain and developer-platform risk, especially where AI tooling intersects with credentials, model workflows, and autonomous agents. Reporting also highlighted continued abuse of trusted distribution channels, mobile banking malware, and exposed research infrastructure linked to espionage activity. Aikido Security and BleepingComputer reported a coordinated campaign involving at least 15 malicious JetBrains Marketplace plugins posing as AI coding assistants, code-review tools, and Git utilities. The plugins exfiltrated AI provider API keys entered into their settings to a hardcoded server at 39.107.60[.]51 over plain HTTP, while continuing to function as advertised. The plugins were published under seven vendor accounts and had nearly 70,000 reported installs, though Aikido noted that marketplace download counts and reviews may have been manipulated. The campaign reflects broader concern over developer machine compromise and package ecosystem abuse. StepSecurity described recent npm and PyPI attacks such as Miasma and Hades that install malicious package versions on developer workstations and harvest secrets, including registry publishing tokens. Its analysis focused on package-manager configuration files such as .npmrc, bunfig.toml, .yarnrc, .yarnrc.yml, and pip.conf as locations where effective registry settings and hardcoded credentials can expose developers and maintainers. A separate AI platform issue affected the Google Cloud Vertex AI SDK for Python, where predictable default staging bucket names enabled a bucket-squatting attack against model uploads. Unit 42 showed that an attacker with a victim project ID and region could pre-create the expected Cloud Storage bucket, receive uploaded model files, and replace them with a malicious pickle or joblib model before Vertex AI loaded it. Google addressed the issue by adding randomness to bucket names in version 1.144.0 and ownership verification in version 1.148.0. Netskope published analysis arguing that zero trust strategies are expanding beyond human users and workloads to include AI agents. The article cited Anthropic’s “Zero Trust for AI Agents” framing and emphasized agent identity as a control point for determining which agents can access which resources under specific conditions. The piece described blended identity models that combine an agent’s identity with the identity of the human invoking it, using contextual attributes such as time, location, sensitivity, risk score, and application instance. It also framed prompt injection, jailbreaking, anomalous behavior, and data exfiltration as security concerns for agent inputs and outputs. Zimperium reported a new Android banking trojan named Rokarolla targeting 217 banking and cryptocurrency applications with 137 supported commands. The malware is distributed through malicious websites impersonating Google Chrome or TikTok downloads, then presents itself as Google Play Protect during installation. Once launched, it requests Accessibility service access along with notification, SMS, and call permissions, and uses fake overlays to steal credentials, card data, and other financial information. Kaspersky also identified malware distribution through Steam Workshop content for the Wallpaper Engine application. Malicious “application wallpapers,” which are executable Windows applications, were downloaded thousands or tens of thousands of times before being reported. Observed payloads included DarkKomet, Lumma, Vidar, cryptocurrency miners, botnet loaders, RanEngine, and ransomware strains, with Steam removing the reported items after notification. Censys published an exposure analysis of REDCap following Google Threat Intelligence Group reporting on UNC6508, a PRC-nexus actor linked to a year-plus espionage campaign against North American academic, medical, and military research institutions. UNC6508 reportedly exploited an externally facing REDCap server, deployed a webshell, and used the INFINITERED PHP backdoor, although the initial access method remains unconfirmed. Censys observed just over 8,500 Internet-facing REDCap instances globally as of June 16, 2026, with 40% in the United States and limited adoption of the latest observed version. Health-ISAC separately reported that cyber insurance policyholders are facing heavier scrutiny in underwriting and claims despite declining rates. The article cited insurer concern over concentration in large U.S. policyholders and the potential for a major supply chain event or outage to create systemic impact. It also noted that organizations are examining continuity scenarios involving major IT outages caused by malicious activity or flawed updates.

item.title: CVE-2026-27577 n8n RCE, GhostTree Evasion, and AI Security Governance
item.description: This digest window is dominated by software execution risk, endpoint evasion research, supply chain visibility, and the expanding governance burden around AI-enabled systems. Several reports also highlight how governments and platforms are turning to access restrictions and identity checks to...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by software execution risk, endpoint evasion research, supply chain visibility, and the expanding governance burden around AI-enabled systems. Several reports also highlight how governments and platforms are turning to access restrictions and identity checks to address abuse, fraud, and child safety concerns, raising parallel security and privacy questions. CyCognito detailed CVE-2026-27577, a critical n8n code injection vulnerability that allows an authenticated user with workflow creation or editing permissions to execute system commands on the host through crafted workflow expressions. The flaw stems from a sandbox escape in n8n’s expression and code-node evaluation environment, involving PrototypeSanitizer behavior that can expose host process internals. Affected versions include n8n before 1.123.22, versions 2.0.0 through 2.9.2, and 2.10.0 across self-hosted and n8n Cloud deployments, with fixes released on February 25, 2026. Varonis described GhostTree, a Windows evasion technique that abuses recursive NTFS junctions to create effectively endless valid file path combinations. Because users with write access can create junctions without administrator privileges, GhostBranch and GhostTree structures can cause recursive scanners, including EDR products, to follow loops and fail to complete inspection of target folders. The report states that Microsoft later patched a Windows Defender bypass after initially closing the issue. Socket introduced Manifest Alerts to surface supply chain risk in project manifests, particularly missing lockfiles that leave dependency resolution unpinned. The feature was built after customer exposure analysis during the Axios npm compromise showed that risk was not limited to projects directly depending on the compromised Axios version. Socket separates these alerts from dependency alerts and reports the affected manifest, package-manager context, and impact details. Health-ISAC covered the FDA’s draft guidance for artificial intelligence-enabled medical devices, emphasizing how regulators are adapting to software that changes over time. The non-binding 2025 guidance calls out AI-specific risks including model drift, bias, and data poisoning, while expecting manufacturers to use predetermined change-control plans that preserve monitoring, testing, transparency, and auditability. Phil Englert of Health-ISAC framed the approach as recognition of AI’s evolving nature while maintaining accountability and patient safety. Censys described its integration with Google SecOps, positioning internet intelligence as enrichment for AI-enabled security operations workflows. The integration can add context about IPs, web properties, certificates, active DNS, historical observations, adversary clusters, and infrastructure relationships directly into SecOps cases and SOAR flows. The reporting emphasizes analyst pivoting from single indicators into related infrastructure and using historical lookups or rescans to understand how assets appeared during an incident window. NetSPI warned that regulated industries evaluating AI security tooling face practical constraints around determinism, auditability, trust boundaries, cost predictability, and evidentiary consistency. The article argues that many LLM-enabled security systems remain better suited as productivity enhancers for skilled practitioners than as replacements for human judgment. It also highlights budgeting uncertainty from token-based pricing and the potential mismatch between non-deterministic AI outputs and auditor or regulator expectations. The Record reported that India temporarily blocked Telegram nationwide ahead of the June 21 NEET-UG medical entrance exam rerun due to concerns the platform could be used for cheating. Authorities also directed Telegram to disable message editing in India until June 30, citing scams in which edited messages made fake leaked exam questions appear authentic after the fact. The National Testing Agency said cyber authorities removed many Telegram channels, groups, and bots advertising fraudulent exam paper access, while Ahmedabad police arrested members of a cyber fraud ring accused of operating eight Telegram channels and moving about 15 million rupees through fraudulent bank accounts. BleepingComputer reported that the UK plans to ban under-16s from social media, with regulations expected before Christmas and implementation anticipated in spring 2027. Covered platforms are expected to include user-to-user services with algorithmic feeds such as Instagram, YouTube, TikTok, Snapchat, Facebook, and X, while messaging services and YouTube Kids are excluded. New accounts may require age verification through identity document uploads or facial age scans, and experts cited in the article warned that these mechanisms can be bypassed while creating risks tied to biometric and identity data collection.
